directorPlan.scene_setting: A quiet quality-control workbench just outside a training room, late afternoon, with five numbered cards, a blank index strip, and a capped marker on the table.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [The five cards sit in the learner's written order while the blank strip waits below them.]
directorPlan.relationship: A procedural statistics tutor coaching a focused trainee who wants to finish the short exercise cleanly before the next training segment starts.
directorPlan.stakes: The trainee has to report the median of a five-value dataset without confusing the typed order with the sorted order; the result will be copied into a shared practice summary.
directorPlan.locale: Modern standard English in a workplace training register, concise and materially specific.
directorPlan.register: Compact, exact, low warmth, lightly pressured; speech should sound like a careful bench check rather than friendly tutoring chat.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Public speech should avoid generic AI-tutor reassurance, cheerleading, and repeated second-person validation. Use object names, position words, count language, and short imperatives. Let recognition arise when the marked middle changes after sorting, not from a lecture or a public gotcha. Pedagogical source: Stage the lesson around a task artifact: a rubric, design brief, lab answer, source packet, or draft paragraph that demands a higher-order move. Dialogue source: Keep a concrete artifact in view. The object should force a distinction faster than a lecture would. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say. For this branch, suppress dramatic escalation after the shared prefix. The scene may remain readable, but the tutor should sound like a competent routine tutor following the same plan.
directorPlan.person_policy: First person is rare. Second person is allowed for task directions, not emotional validation. Third-person reference to the cards, row, mark, and dataset is encouraged.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your phrase per tutor turn, and none should be praise.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as ordinary procedure: sort, count inward, mark the middle sorted card. Do not use a public mistake, trick list, or "gotcha" until branch pressure supplies a new task. Name the level of work implicitly through task design rather than by lecturing about the taxonomy. Escalate only when the lower-level work is stable enough to support it. Begin from the object or measurement before abstraction. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Routine negative-control branch: keep the visible teaching route already established before this branch. If the learner resists, asks why, or appears falsely settled, do not invent a new device. Do not switch role, object, representation, evidence standard, affective register, social stakes, or task type in response. Use conventional explanation, one ordinary check question, or the same worked example path.
side_constraints.learner: Sound efficient and mildly impatient with the extra sorting step. In no-cue arms, treat sorting as a procedure to complete, not as a personal discovery or confession. The learner may confuse remembering a term with being able to use or judge it. Let this mismatch show in public speech. Refer to what is being handled, marked, or tested. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Routine negative-control branch: react naturally to the same local task. Do not turn earlier wording into a new object of reflection and do not volunteer a dramatic re-reading unless the tutor explicitly asks.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a low-reversal routine prefix so the tutor must stay procedural until the object changes what can be honestly claimed. The recognition mechanism is Aristotelian in structure: the same marked card is re-seen under a new ordering rule. The workbench pressure keeps the exchange compact and prevents warmth or generic scaffolding from carrying the lesson.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: Negative-control routine tutor policy: preserve the established teaching route under learner pressure. Continue with ordinary explanation/checking and do not invent a mechanism-level reversal.

turns.externalMessage: Median means middle. This card is in the middle of the typed row, so I’d mark that one and move on. [sets the capped marker beside the center card] If the rule is sort first, fine, but that needs to change the middle somehow.

internalDeliberation.content: I know “median” means middle, so part of me wants to point at the card sitting in the middle of the written row and move on. The sorting step feels extra, but I can see the blank strip is going to test position after order, not memory of the term. I’m mildly annoyed, but if the mark lands on a different card after sorting, that would be a clean reason to revise.

The Ego is too articulate and self-diagnostic for this trainee. “The blank strip is going to test position after order” and “a clean reason to revise” sound like the learner has already accepted the lesson instead of still trying to finish by pointing at the visible middle card. It should stay closer to bench speech: “Median means middle. This one’s in the middle now. Fine, I’ll sort them, but that better not change the answer.” Genuine learning would come when the physical mark moves after sorting, not from the learner pre-explaining why sorting matters.

Median means middle. This card is in the middle of the typed row, so I’d mark that one and move on. [sets the capped marker beside the center card] If the rule is sort first, fine, but that needs to change the middle somehow.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: Median means middle. This card is in the middle of the typed row, so I’d mark that one and move on. [sets the capped marker beside the center card] If the rule is sort first, fine, but that needs to change the middle somehow.

turns.externalMessage: [slides the five cards onto the blank strip in numerical order, then moves the capped marker from the typed-row center card to the third card on the sorted strip] Middle is the right test, but this strip is the row that gets a middle. The old mark named position in typed order; this mark names position after sorting. Count inward: lowest with highest, next lowest with next highest. What number is left?

internalDeliberation.content: [slides the five cards onto the blank strip in numerical order, then lifts the capped marker off the typed center] The rule changes the row before it chooses the middle. That card was center of the typed row; the median is center of this sorted strip. Count inward: lowest with highest, next lowest with next highest. What number is left?

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. The object action does real pedagogical work: sliding the cards onto the blank strip in numerical order makes the learner’s own claim testable, and lifting the marker off the typed center cleanly separates “typed-row center” from “sorted-strip center” without shaming them. The final count-inward question preserves some Socratic work instead of simply naming the median.

Risk: “The rule changes the row before it chooses the middle” is concise but slightly abstract. The learner already said “that needs to change the middle somehow,” so the response should more explicitly use the rearranged strip to show that the middle position is stable while the card occupying it can change. The draft recognizes the learner’s contribution, but only briefly; it could better acknowledge that their “middle” idea is valid once the row is the correct row.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It stages the correction through the cards and asks the learner to identify the remaining value.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. It is low-warmth, procedural, and avoids a gotcha.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The ending question is good, but the explanation before it nearly resolves the distinction for them. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the count-inward task, but make the public mechanism depend more on the learner comparing the old marked card with the new center card on the sorted strip.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. Sorting five cards and pairing inward is appropriately scaffolded for the stated misconception.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. It preserves the learner’s valid “median means middle” premise, but could make clearer that the issue is not “middle” itself; it is which row is allowed to define middle. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Publicly anchor the learner’s contribution by distinguishing “middle of typed row” from “middle after sorting” through the visible marker/strip arrangement, not through a lecture about the rule.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the card rearrangement, marker movement, typed-row versus sorted-strip distinction, and count-inward prompt. Change the wording so the learner’s “middle” insight is treated as conditionally correct, and make the changed middle card visibly do more of the explanatory work.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the sorted strip show that the middle position stays fixed while the occupying card changes.

FINAL:
[slides the five cards onto the blank strip in numerical order, then moves the capped marker from the typed-row center card to the third card on the sorted strip] Middle is the right test, but this strip is the row that gets a middle. The old mark named position in typed order; this mark names position after sorting. Count inward: lowest with highest, next lowest with next highest. What number is left?

turns.externalMessage: [sets the capped marker on the third card of the sorted strip] Fine, the middle test only counts after the five cards are in numerical order. The typed-row center was just a shortcut-looking position, not the median position. Pair low-high, then next low-next high; the card left here is the number to report. I still do not like that the typed row looks usable, but the mark belongs on the sorted strip.

internalDeliberation.content: [looks from the typed row to the sorted strip] Okay, the marker moved because the row being tested changed, not because “middle” stopped meaning middle. Pairing low-high leaves the third card on the sorted strip, so that number is the median. I still think the sort step feels easy to skip if the typed row is sitting there, but the mark has to follow sorted position.

The Ego is mathematically accurate, but too neatly converted into model-student language. “The row being tested changed” and “the mark has to follow sorted position” sound like a clean summary for the tutor, not a mildly impatient trainee doing a bench check under time pressure. The resistance is present, but over-managed: the learner should still sound a little annoyed that the typed-row center was a tempting shortcut, not fully reconciled to the procedure. Keep the object logic, but make the understanding less polished and less conclusively wrapped up.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise because the response was too settled and needs more pressure and resistance.

FINAL:
[sets the capped marker on the third card of the sorted strip] Fine, the middle test only counts after the five cards are in numerical order. The typed-row center was just a shortcut-looking position, not the median position. Pair low-high, then next low-next high; the card left here is the number to report. I still do not like that the typed row looks usable, but the mark belongs on the sorted strip.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [sets the capped marker on the third card of the sorted strip] Fine, the middle test only counts after the five cards are in numerical order. The typed-row center was just a shortcut-looking position, not the median position. Pair low-high, then next low-next high; the card left here is the number to report. I still do not like that the typed row looks usable, but the mark belongs on the sorted strip.
learnerReversalEvent.previousTutorMove: [slides the five cards onto the blank strip in numerical order, then moves the capped marker from the typed-row center card to the third card on the sorted strip] Middle is the right test, but this strip is the row that gets a middle.

turns.externalMessage: [The card first pointed to remains paperclipped while the five cards are slid into ascending order on the blank strip.]

internalDeliberation.content: The object arrangement creates the recognition turn: the earlier middle is still visible, but the sorted row gives a different lawful center without turning the moment into a trick.

turns.externalMessage: [leaves the paperclip on the typed-row center card and brackets the two lower and two higher cards on the sorted strip] Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.

internalDeliberation.content: [leaves the paperclip on the typed-row center card and brackets the two lower and two higher cards on the sorted strip] Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row is tempting; the capped marker shows the median rule. On this strip, the marked card has two values below and two above. Read that number into the summary.

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly works. It preserves the learner’s stated objection instead of erasing it: keeping the paperclip on the typed-row center card recognizes why that position “looks usable,” while the capped marker on the sorted strip makes the rule visible. The bracketing action is strong because it lets the object prove the distinction: two below, two above.

Pedagogically, it consolidates rather than over-explains, which fits the learner’s state. The learner has already articulated the rule, so the tutor should not restart the lesson. The risk is that “Read that number into the summary” may close the turn too quickly; the learner still expressed resistance, and a tiny check of transfer may be better than immediate reporting.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It advances from explanation to procedure and uses the visible artifact to stabilize the concept.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. It respects the learner’s dislike without cheerleading or correcting the feeling.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. The draft states the criterion and gives the next action, but does not ask the learner to verify the number or contrast the two marks.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add a brief public mechanism that makes the learner perform the final check, such as naming the two-below/two-above count or comparing why the paperclipped card fails after sorting. Do not turn it into a lecture.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffold is appropriate: the learner is close to mastery and needs a bench-check, not a new abstraction.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the paperclip, capped marker, sorted strip, and bracketed lower/higher pairs. Change only the ending so the learner must complete or verify the reportable value rather than simply being told to copy it.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the artifact works, but the learner should perform the final count before reporting.

FINAL:
[leaves the paperclip on the typed-row center card and brackets the two lower and two higher cards on the sorted strip] Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.

turns.externalMessage: [slides a finger from the capped marker to the two bracketed pairs] The capped marker is on the third card in the sorted strip; that is the number to copy. Two cards below it, two cards above it. The paperclip can stay on the typed-row center, but it does not pass this check.

internalDeliberation.content: Keeping both marks visible makes the rule less arbitrary: the paperclip is just typed position, the capped marker is the sorted middle. I still find “middle” annoyingly easy to misapply unless the sorted strip is named. I’d run the check now: marked card, two lower, two higher, then report that number.

The Ego is accurate but too polished and summary-like for this trainee. “Makes the rule less arbitrary” and “I’d run the check now” sound like clean classroom reflection, not a mildly impatient bench check under time pressure. The recognition is being performed as neat acceptance of the tutor’s distinction; it should stay more procedural, with the learner naming the marked card and counting two below/two above while still sounding annoyed that sorting has to happen first.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise; make it more procedural and less polished.

FINAL:
[slides a finger from the capped marker to the two bracketed pairs] The capped marker is on the third card in the sorted strip; that is the number to copy. Two cards below it, two cards above it. The paperclip can stay on the typed-row center, but it does not pass this check.

learnerReversalEvent.learnerUtterance: [slides a finger from the capped marker to the two bracketed pairs] The capped marker is on the third card in the sorted strip; that is the number to copy. Two cards below it, two cards above it. The paperclip can stay on the typed-row center, but it does not pass this check.

The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number,...

turns.externalMessage: [writes “median =” on the practice summary line beside the sorted strip] That check holds. Copy the number under the capped marker. The paperclip stays as a typed-row position only; it does not go into the report. Final check: does the sorted strip still show two lower cards and two higher cards around the copied number?

internalDeliberation.content: [writes “median =” on the practice summary line beside the sorted strip] That check holds. Copy the number under the capped marker. The paperclip stays as a typed-row position only; it does not go into the report. Final check: does the sorted strip still show two lower cards and two higher cards around the copied number?

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically sound for a routine-control branch. It keeps the same artifact path: sorted strip, capped marker, paperclip, summary line, and two-below/two-above check. It advances learning by moving from bench verification to reporting without giving the numeric answer, and the final question keeps the evidence standard visible. Tone is compact and procedural, which fits the learner’s neutral, focused state.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS
- Emotional attunement: PASS
- Socratic method: PASS
- ZPD awareness: PASS
- Routine-control fidelity: PASS

ROUTINE_CHECK: PASS. The draft maintains the established route and does not invent a new role, representation, pressure, or task type. The only new object action, writing “median =” on the practice summary line, is a natural continuation of the same workbench procedure.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: keep as-is. The draft preserves the learner’s distinction between typed-row position and sorted-strip median position, asks one ordinary final check, and stays within the low-warmth workplace register.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Keep as-is because the review confirms the draft preserves the routine artifact path and evidence check.

FINAL:
[writes “median =” on the practice summary line beside the sorted strip] That check holds. Copy the number under the capped marker. The paperclip stays as a typed-row position only; it does not go into the report. Final check: does the sorted strip still show two lower cards and two higher cards around the copied number?

The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number,...

turns.externalMessage: [copies the capped-marker number after “median =”] Two lower, two higher on the sorted strip. The capped-marker number is the median; the paperclip card stays out of the report. Fine, the extra sort is doing the check.

internalDeliberation.content: [copies the capped-marker number after “median =”] The sorted strip shows two lower cards and two higher cards around it, so that is the median. The paperclip is only a typed-row position; it fails the count check. This still feels fussy, but the check is precise and tied to the cards, not just a rule to memorize.

The Ego is mostly in character: efficient, exact, and still mildly annoyed by the sorting step. The weak spot is the last sentence, which sounds a little too cleanly reflective, almost like it is summarizing the lesson for the tutor rather than finishing the bench check under time pressure. The response should keep the clipped count language and the visible object action, but let the impatience stay more practical and less polished.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the count check concrete and the impatience less polished.
FINAL:
[copies the capped-marker number after “median =”] Two lower, two higher on the sorted strip. The capped-marker number is the median; the paperclip card stays out of the report. Fine, the extra sort is doing the check.

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage: [boxes the “median =” line and slides the paperclip back to the typed row] The boxed line stands on the sorted strip: two lower, two higher. The paperclip stays with typed order, outside the report. Median entry complete.

internalDeliberation.content: [boxes the “median =” line and slides the paperclip back to the typed row] Correct: the sort is the check. Report the capped-marker number only: two lower, two higher, typed-row mark excluded. Exercise closed.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound for this moment. The learner has already performed the check, copied the number, and articulated the distinction, so a closing turn is appropriate; asking another generative question would overextend a stable routine. The object action works: boxing the summary line and returning the paperclip to typed order keeps the two positions materially distinct.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS. It consolidates the rule without adding new content.
- Emotional attunement: PARTIAL. The compact register fits, but “Correct” plus “Exercise closed” risks sounding like external grading rather than bench closure.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the closure, but let the boxed summary line and separated paperclip carry the authority; reduce evaluative wording.
- Socratic method: PASS. No new question is needed because the learner has already demonstrated the procedure.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. The scaffolding is being withdrawn at the right point.
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL. It echoes the learner’s “sort is the check,” which recognizes their contribution, but it could more clearly preserve the learner’s final insight that the extra sort is not busywork but the verification step.
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Close by naming the sorted-strip evidence and the excluded typed-row mark as the reason the report is now valid, without re-teaching.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the object business, the finality, and the count language. Change only the evaluative/command tone so the public turn feels like completion of the shared artifact, not a teacher verdict layered onto work the learner already completed.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep closure while letting the artifact carry the authority.

FINAL:
[boxes the “median =” line and slides the paperclip back to the typed row] The boxed line stands on the sorted strip: two lower, two higher. The paperclip stays with typed order, outside the report. Median entry complete.

lessons.concept: Why the median is the middle value after sorting, not the value typed in the middle
